figures.caption: Table 2. Comparison with state-of-the-art methods on the OV-LVIS dataset [14]. To ensure a fair comparison, all listed methods employ RCNN-style detectors [16]. We report the mean and standard deviation across 3 independent runs. † indicates results obtained from our re-implementation.

Figure 2. Overview of our framework for OVD, which mainly consists of three steps. First, a set of candidate regions is extracted from an image using an RPN. For each candidate region, we employ the HCC technique for selectively assigning a class label, while LoCLIP estimates an objectness score. Pseudo labels are assigned only to regions with hierarchically consistent predictions and sufficiently high objectness scores. These pseudo labels, together with ground-truth annotations for base object classes, are then used to train an OV detector with classification and regression losses. See the main text for details.

Figure 3. An illustration of the operations in the HCC technique for an input image (right), where novel object classes are defined as dog and cat, and K is set to 2. Here, the class label estimation from the class level and the sub-category level are consistent for dog, represented in green nodes. Consequently, the HCC technique boosts the confidence score from max(p) = 0.6 to max(rsub) = 0.79. See text for details.

Figure 4. Illustration of LoCLIP. LoCLIP appends a learnable [OBJ] token into a pretrained ViT encoder of CLIP. The output feature for the [OBJ] token is passed through a FC layer, and predicts an objectness score of an input patch xb. Throughout the adaptation process, all pretrained components, including the [CLS] token, embedding layers and ViT encoders, are kept frozen.
